original_reasoning: Arabic السفه is foolish, rash, irreverent behavior; Greek βλάσφημος is abusive or impious speech. Both meet in irreverent moral/intellectual disorder.
Arabic includes coaxing away and extreme foolishness, and Greek means chatter or babble; both converge on unstable, speech-linked behavior.
Arabic roughness or barren coarseness maps well onto Greek 'ugly, shameful' through a shared negative-quality core.
آزاج suggests arching or paired joining, which fits a yoking or linkage sense well.
Farʿawnah carries cunning and pharaonic harshness, while oloophron is destructive-minded; both share a strong negative mental quality with partial phonetic convergence around fr/n-phron.
Arabic kind or type of a thing aligns fairly well with Greek sample or specimen.
الحضير is gathered matter; rubbed groats are also a gathered granular mass, a plausible concrete match.
Arabic explicitly includes very sweet water, and Greek means carrying water for bathing, so water is a direct shared core.
